findings.thai: เขียนชื่อไว้
findings.reason: The word 'ไว้' (wai) has a low-class initial consonant (ว) with a tone mark 2 (-้), which produces a high tone, not a falling tone.
The word 'เยอะ' (yoe) has a low-class initial consonant (ย) with a short vowel and dead ending, which produces a high tone, not a low tone.
The word 'ครั้ง' (khrang) has a low-class initial consonant (ค) with a tone mark 2 (-้), which produces a high tone, not a falling tone.
The word 'ตอบ' (top) has a mid-class initial consonant (ต) with a dead ending (บ), which produces a low tone, not a high tone.
The word 'คิด' (khit) has a low-class initial consonant (ค) with a short vowel and dead ending (ด), which produces a high tone, not a falling tone.
The word 'คืน' (khuen) has a low-class initial consonant (ค) with a live ending (น) and no tone mark, which produces a mid tone, not a falling tone.
The word 'เงียบ' (ngiap) has a low-class initial consonant (ง) with a long vowel and dead ending (บ), which produces a falling tone, not a low tone.
The word 'ใหม่' (mai) has a high-class leading consonant (ห) with a tone mark 1 (-่), which produces a low tone, not a falling tone.
The word 'เช้า' (chao) has a low-class initial consonant (ช) with a tone mark 2 (-้), which produces a high tone, not a falling tone.
The second syllable 'นน' (non) follows the high-class initial 'ถ', acting as if it has a leading 'ห', which gives it a rising tone, not a mid tone.
The word 'ก็' (gaw) is a special exception in Thai and is pronounced with a falling tone, not a low tone.
The word 'อย่าง' (yaang) has a mid-class leading consonant (อ) with a tone mark 1 (-่), which produces a low tone, not a falling tone.
